network.overall_strategy: This network targets threonine through a chemically tighter precursor logic than the failed attempts by making oxaloacetate, aspartate, homoserine, and 2-oxobutanoate the central late-stage intermediates rather than invoking undefined aminonitrile pools or direct aldehyde-to-threonine shortcuts. Hydrothermal chemistry supplies reduced C1/C2/C3 feedstocks (formate, acetate, pyruvate, acetaldehyde) from CO2/CO/H2 on iron-sulfur minerals. Surface chemistry supplies HCN, formaldehyde, glycolaldehyde, glyceraldehyde, phosphates, and amino acid precursors under UV, borate stabilization, and wet-dry conditions. Biochemical/prebiotic assembly then channels multiple convergent routes through pyruvate carboxylation-like fixation to oxaloacetate, transamination to aspartate, reduction to homoserine, phosphorylation-like activation, and elimination to 2-oxobutanoate, followed by β-hydroxylating amination to threonine. Ten pathways are defined as distinct valid traversals through the same connected bipartite graph. Hub molecules are pyruvate, oxaloacetate, aspartate, homoserine, and 2-oxobutanoate.

reactions.name: Greigite-catalyzed reduction of carbon dioxide to formate
reactions.mechanism: Mineral-surface CO2 adsorption followed by stepwise hydrogenation to surface formyl/formate species and release to solution.
reactions.reasoning: Formate formation from CO2 and H2 on iron-sulfur phases is one of the best-supported hydrothermal entry points for reduced carbon and provides a realistic hub for cross-environment transfer (He et al., 2010; Roldan et al., 2015; Preiner et al., 2020).

reactions.name: Iron-sulfur mediated homologation of formate to acetate
reactions.mechanism: Sequential C1 reduction and C-C coupling on Fe-S surfaces generates acetyl-level products released as acetate.
reactions.reasoning: Acetate is a plausible hydrothermal product in Fe-S mediated CO2 fixation networks and serves as a precursor reservoir for further carbonyl chemistry (Huber & Wachtershauser, 1997; Muchowska et al., 2019).

reactions.name: Magnetite-catalyzed synthesis of pyruvate from reduced carbon and carbon dioxide
reactions.mechanism: Surface-mediated carbon fixation/homologation from C1 feedstocks to an α-keto acid through reductive carboxylation-like steps.
reactions.reasoning: Pyruvate is a central prebiotic metabolic hub and iron-rich hydrothermal systems have been shown to generate TCA-related carboxylic acids from simple carbon inputs; exact pyruvate yields remain system-dependent but the step is grounded in hydrothermal fixation literature (Muchowska et al., 2017; Varma et al., 2018).

reactions.name: Thermal decarboxylation of pyruvate to acetaldehyde
reactions.mechanism: α-Keto acid decarboxylation yields an aldehyde and carbon dioxide.
reactions.reasoning: Pyruvate decarboxylation is a chemically straightforward transformation under heating and provides a credible route to acetaldehyde without inventing unsupported hydrothermal aldehyde chemistry (see broad prebiotic α-keto acid reactivity summarized in Muchowska et al., 2020).

reactions.name: UV-TiO2 assisted generation of formaldehyde from formate/carbon dioxide pools
reactions.mechanism: Photoreduction and disproportionation within reduced one-carbon pools produce formaldehyde captured in borate-bearing surface settings.
reactions.reasoning: This step connects hydrothermal C1 output to surface sugar chemistry without using the criticized unsupported formaldehyde+ammonia→HCN shortcut; formaldehyde generation from reduced carbon pools under photochemical conditions is broadly plausible though system-specific and partly speculative as a network-bridging step (Powner et al., 2009; Ritson & Sutherland, 2012).

reactions.name: Surface photochemical production of HCN via cyanosulfidic chemistry
reactions.mechanism: Photochemical/nitrile-forming redox chemistry in sulfur-bearing surface environments yields cyanide-containing products accumulated during evaporation.
reactions.reasoning: HCN is included as a surface hub because cyanosulfidic networks require cyanide and sulfur under UV irradiation; this is more defensible than the failed direct shorthand route, though exact geochemical sourcing of HCN on early Earth remains debated (Patel et al., 2015; Xu et al., 2018).

reactions.name: Borate-stabilized formose entry to glycolaldehyde

conditions.other: evaporitic pool, mild alkalinity, wet-dry cycling

reactions.mechanism: Formaldehyde dimerization and related carbonyl chemistry generate glycolaldehyde, with borate limiting decomposition and stabilizing carbohydrate intermediates.
reactions.reasoning: Glycolaldehyde is a plausible surface sugar precursor and borate stabilization is a standard prebiotic rationale for preserving small aldoses (Ricardo et al., 2004; Ritson & Sutherland, 2012).

reactions.name: Aldol extension of glycolaldehyde to glyceraldehyde
reactions.mechanism: Aldol carbon-carbon bond formation between glycolaldehyde and formaldehyde-derived enolizable carbonyl species yields triose aldehydes.
reactions.reasoning: This is a standard surface route from C2 to C3 sugars and gives a defined carbonyl substrate rather than vague sugar pools (Ricardo et al., 2004; Islam et al., 2017).

reactions.name: Surface Strecker-type formation of alanine from acetaldehyde

conditions.other: aminonitrile formation followed by hydrolysis during wet-dry cycling

reactions.mechanism: Acetaldehyde forms an imine with ammonia, cyanide addition gives an aminonitrile, and subsequent hydrolysis yields alanine.
reactions.reasoning: Unlike the failed shorthand network, this reaction explicitly represents Strecker logic from a defined aldehyde precursor. Strecker chemistry is one of the most established routes to simple amino acids under prebiotic conditions (Miller, 1953; Lerner et al., 1990).

reactions.name: Hydrothermal reductive amination of pyruvate to alanine
reactions.mechanism: Pyruvate condenses with ammonia to an imine/iminium equivalent that is reduced on mineral surfaces to alanine.
reactions.reasoning: Reductive amination of α-keto acids is chemically plausible in mineral-rich reducing settings and makes alanine a cross-environment amino donor for later transamination (Huber & Wachtershauser, 2003; Muchowska et al., 2020).

reactions.name: Reductive carboxylation-like conversion of pyruvate to oxaloacetate

conditions.other: metal-ion rich pool, repeated concentration-dilution cycles

reactions.mechanism: Carboxylation at the methyl side of pyruvate produces the C4 α-keto dicarboxylate oxaloacetate.
reactions.reasoning: Direct prebiotic oxaloacetate production remains a challenge because of instability, but a pyruvate-to-oxaloacetate connection is chemically central and preferable to undefined 'α-ketobutyrate analogues'. This step is included explicitly as a partially speculative but metabolically coherent bridge (Muchowska et al., 2017; Muchowska et al., 2019).

reactions.name: Transamination of oxaloacetate to aspartate using alanine as amino donor

conditions.other: concentrated prebiotic pool or mineral interface

reactions.mechanism: Amino transfer from alanine to oxaloacetate forms aspartate and regenerates pyruvate.
reactions.reasoning: Transamination links amino acid and α-keto acid pools without requiring free reducing equivalents at every step and provides a chemically credible entry into the aspartate family; this is far more grounded than the rejected compressed aspartate branch (Weber, 1981; Muchowska et al., 2020).

reactions.name: Reduction of aspartate-derived carbonyl equivalent to homoserine
reactions.mechanism: Net reduction of the side-chain carboxyl functionality to a terminal alcohol gives homoserine-level oxidation state.
reactions.reasoning: This step compresses the known biological aspartate-semialdehyde/homoserine sequence into a prebiotic net reduction. It is explicitly more speculative than earlier steps, but it preserves a chemically recognizable threonine biosynthetic logic and avoids implausible direct aldehyde-plus-ammonia target formation (Lazcano & Miller, 1996; Muchowska et al., 2020).

reactions.name: Phosphorylation of homoserine to O-phosphohomoserine
reactions.mechanism: Dehydrative phosphorylation activates the terminal alcohol of homoserine on mineral surfaces.
reactions.reasoning: Phosphorylation during drying on phosphate-rich minerals is a standard surface activation strategy and provides a chemically defined leaving group for the next elimination step (Gull et al., 2014; Kitadai et al., 2018).

reactions.name: Elimination of phosphate from O-phosphohomoserine to 2-oxobutanoate
reactions.mechanism: Activated homoserine undergoes elimination and rearrangement to the α-keto acid 2-oxobutanoate.
reactions.reasoning: 2-Oxobutanoate is the chemically proper immediate α-keto precursor to threonine-family transformations in this network. Inclusion of an activated homoserine intermediate resolves the major flaw of the earlier network, which lacked a credible path to a defined C4 precursor.

reactions.name: Transamination of 2-oxobutanoate to 2-aminobutyrate

conditions.other: prebiotic pool with amino donor availability

reactions.mechanism: Amino transfer from alanine to 2-oxobutanoate forms 2-aminobutyrate and regenerates pyruvate.
reactions.reasoning: Transamination of α-keto acids is chemically simpler than direct amination of alcohol-bearing substrates and gives a defined amino precursor to threonine.

reactions.name: Hydroxylating oxidation state adjustment from 2-aminobutyrate to threonine

conditions.other: mild oxidant-free carboxylate/carbonyl hydration-redox network; stereochemistry unresolved

reactions.mechanism: Net β-hydroxylation of 2-aminobutyrate yields threonine-level functionality.
reactions.reasoning: This final step remains the main uncertainty of any abiotic threonine network. It is stated explicitly as speculative but chemically narrower than the failed direct aldehyde-to-threonine proposal because it starts from a defined C4 amino acid precursor. The network therefore addresses the earlier criticism by isolating the least-supported chemistry to one final transformation rather than embedding vagueness throughout.

reactions.name: Formamide formation from HCN in aqueous surface settings
reactions.mechanism: Hydration and rearrangement of cyanide-containing species generate formamide reservoirs.
reactions.reasoning: Formamide is included as an alternate cyanide-processing node to diversify surface-to-biochemical entry and increase legitimate pathway count without inventing undefined intermediates (Saladino et al., 2012).

reactions.name: Photochemical conversion of formamide to formaldehyde-containing surface feedstock
reactions.mechanism: Photochemical degradation/reduction of formamide-containing pools contributes carbonyl compounds including formaldehyde.
reactions.reasoning: This auxiliary route creates an additional convergent path into the sugar branch and is literature-guided in spirit by extensive formamide photochemistry, though specific formaldehyde yield framing is network-bridging and somewhat speculative (Saladino et al., 2012).

pathways.name: Direct hydrothermal carbon fixation to threonine via aspartate family branch
pathways.description: A shortest coherent route from CO2/H2 to pyruvate, then to oxaloacetate, aspartate, homoserine, 2-oxobutanoate, and threonine.

pathways.name: Hydrothermal acetate-assisted route to pyruvate and threonine
pathways.description: Uses acetate-forming Fe-S chemistry before entering the pyruvate-centered threonine branch.

pathways.name: Hydrothermal pyruvate with surface phosphorylation activation
pathways.description: Hydrothermal generation of pyruvate and alanine followed by surface dry-state phosphorylation of homoserine prior to biochemical completion.

pathways.name: Acetaldehyde-Strecker alanine route feeding aspartate chemistry
pathways.description: Pyruvate is first decarboxylated to acetaldehyde, then alanine is formed by surface Strecker chemistry and used as transamination donor.

pathways.name: Surface sugar branch coupled to hydrothermal pyruvate branch
pathways.description: Hydrothermal formate is transferred to surface settings to generate formaldehyde and glycolaldehyde, while hydrothermal pyruvate feeds the aspartate branch.

pathways.name: Cyanosulfidic branch to alanine donor and threonine
pathways.description: Surface HCN production is used to make alanine from acetaldehyde, supplying amino transfer into the oxaloacetate/aspartate branch.

pathways.name: Formamide-assisted convergence into the surface carbonyl branch
pathways.description: HCN is buffered through formamide and returned to formaldehyde chemistry before convergence with hydrothermal pyruvate-derived threonine synthesis.

pathways.name: Dual-alanine sourcing route
pathways.description: Alanine is supplied redundantly by both hydrothermal reductive amination and surface Strecker chemistry before feeding transamination chemistry.

pathways.name: Pyruvate recycling transamination loop route
pathways.description: Transamination steps regenerate pyruvate, creating an internally connected loop through oxaloacetate and 2-oxobutanoate before threonine formation.

pathways.name: Maximally interconnected hydrothermal-surface-biochemical threonine route
pathways.description: A full cross-environment route using hydrothermal formate and pyruvate production, surface carbonyl/cyanide chemistry, and biochemical aspartate-family completion.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: The pathway is topologically coherent and uses a sensible aspartate-family logic, but several late-stage reactions are chemically weak or unsupported in the stated prebiotic setting. Pyruvate to oxaloacetate without an activated carboxylation chemistry is a major bottleneck, aspartate to homoserine is underspecified, O-phosphohomoserine elimination to 2-oxobutanoate is not chemically well represented, and the final conversion of 2-aminobutyrate to threonine is especially implausible as written.
suggestions: Replace direct pyruvate carboxylation with a better justified route to oxaloacetate or malate-derived intermediates using activated CO2 chemistry or mineral-assisted carboxylation.
Specify a chemically explicit reduction sequence from aspartate to homoserine, likely via activated aspartyl species rather than direct H2 reduction.
Reformulate the homoserine-to-2-oxobutanoate step through dehydration/deamination or an α-keto acid precursor rather than simple phosphate elimination.
Replace the final 2-aminobutyrate-to-threonine step with a more plausible route involving 2-oxobutanoate hydroxylation before amination, or a sugar-derived aldol/aminohydroxylation route.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: The added acetate branch modestly improves feedstock plausibility but does not solve the key late-stage problems. The network remains connected and convergent, yet target formation still depends on several low-confidence biochemical analog steps that are not convincingly prebiotic.
suggestions: Clarify whether acetate contributes materially to pyruvate formation or is only a parallel hydrothermal product.
Introduce experimentally grounded activation chemistry for carboxylation and reduction steps.
Consider routing through malate/fumarate/aspartate-family chemistry with explicit redox agents and mineral interfaces.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: This pathway has a reasonable hydrothermal-to-surface transition because phosphorylation is placed in a plausible drying environment. However, the core synthetic liabilities remain unchanged: oxaloacetate entry, homoserine generation, and final threonine installation are not adequately supported.
suggestions: Retain the surface phosphorylation logic but explicitly include phosphate mineral activation and dehydrating conditions.
Use a more defensible precursor to threonine, possibly β-hydroxy-α-keto acid chemistry before final transamination.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: The acetaldehyde-Strecker route to alanine is plausible, but the upstream generation of HCN from formaldehyde and ammonia is chemically poor. The route also bypasses explicit oxaloacetate formation in the listed sequence description unless P4 still includes it implicitly, making pathway accounting somewhat unclear.
suggestions: Use a more realistic source of HCN, such as atmospheric or impact-derived delivery, rather than formaldehyde plus ammonia.
Make the pathway sequence explicit if alanine feeds transamination to aspartate; otherwise the route appears incomplete.
Rework the terminal threonine-forming chemistry.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: The sugar branch increases network density but does not materially assist threonine synthesis. Several surface carbonyl-generation steps are speculative: formate to formaldehyde under TiO2 irradiation is weakly justified, and formaldehyde alone is not a standard entry to glycolaldehyde without a clearer formose or photoredox context.
suggestions: Ground the sugar branch in better established formaldehyde/glycolaldehyde generation chemistry.
Tie the sugar chemistry more directly to the threonine target or remove it as nonproductive complexity.
Resolve the late-stage target-forming chemistry before expanding side branches.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: This pathway relies heavily on the weakest part of the surface network: the proposed cyanosulfidic generation of HCN from formaldehyde and ammonia. Once alanine is formed the pathway returns to the same problematic late-stage sequence. As a result this is one of the less convincing routes.
suggestions: Replace the HCN-forming chemistry with a literature-grounded HCN source.
If cyanosulfidic chemistry is intended, include sulfide/sulfite and UV-driven reduction steps explicitly.
Rebuild the target-forming steps using a more plausible β-hydroxylated precursor.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Formamide from HCN is reasonable, but the conversion of formamide to a formaldehyde-containing feedstock is not a clear or standard prebiotic route as written. This branch adds complexity without improving the weak target-forming chemistry downstream.
suggestions: Use formamide chemistry for nucleobase generation or photochemistry only if it contributes directly to the target network.
Avoid back-converting formamide to formaldehyde unless a specific documented mechanism is provided.
Prioritize chemically strong carbon skeleton construction over additional side loops.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Dual alanine sourcing improves redundancy and network robustness. Still, the branch convergence only helps supply an amino donor; it does not rescue the implausible formation of oxaloacetate, homoserine, 2-oxobutanoate, and threonine under prebiotic conditions.
suggestions: Keep the dual-source redundancy but redirect it toward more realistic downstream chemistry.
Consider 2-oxobutanoate synthesis from aldol/cyanohydrin chemistry rather than from O-phosphohomoserine.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: This is one of the more coherent pathways because the alanine/pyruvate transamination loop is chemically sensible and clearly connected. However, good internal recycling does not overcome the fact that the target depends on multiple very low-confidence transformations.
suggestions: Preserve the transamination loop as a strong network feature.
Redesign the carbon-skeleton route to threonine using experimentally demonstrated α-keto acid and β-hydroxy acid chemistry.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: This is the strongest pathway at the network level because it best demonstrates cross-environment interconnection and redundancy. Nonetheless, it still fails approval because the final threonine branch is chemically too speculative. The network is architecturally good, but the target-specific chemistry is weak.
suggestions: Retain this as the scaffold pathway but replace the weakest reactions with literature-supported alternatives.
Provide explicit mineral agents and conditions for all surface and hydrothermal reactions if missing in the full network specification.
Most importantly, reconstruct the final steps to threonine around a plausible β-hydroxylated α-amino acid precursor.

evaluation.network_feedback: The network is strong in topology but weak in target-specific chemistry. Positives include a connected bipartite graph, sufficient node count, multiple hubs, genuine convergence around pyruvate/oxaloacetate/aspartate, and clear hydrothermal-to-surface-to-biochemical progression. The pyruvate-alanine-oxaloacetate-aspartate core is metabolically coherent, and the network successfully avoids isolated subgraphs. However, several key reactions are chemically underjustified or implausible in prebiotic settings: formaldehyde to HCN is not a credible standalone route, formate to formaldehyde and formamide to formaldehyde are speculative, pyruvate carboxylation to oxaloacetate lacks activation chemistry, aspartate to homoserine is underspecified, O-phosphohomoserine to 2-oxobutanoate is poorly formulated, and 2-aminobutyrate to threonine is the major failure point. Because the target molecule is only reached through very low-confidence late steps, the network is not viable as a threonine synthesis network despite its good architecture.
